stories.title: The Tide-Thief's Gambit
stories.text:
Nara the Tide-Thief had eluded the deepkin for three years—until they cornered her in Vel-Karsh harbor. She stood on the dock as their singers rose from the depths, webbed hands grasping at air, their song like drowning backward. The deepkin captain, a scarred thing with pearls for teeth, offered her a choice: the gallows, or the Sunken Well.

"You stole our gold," the captain sang. "You stole our song. Now you will steal for us."

Nara laughed, tasting salt and desperation. "What's left to steal?"

"The Stellar Charts from the High Observatory of Karethil. Guarded by the Sovereign's own mages." The captain's smile was a weapon. "Recover them within the moon, and we feed you to the depths ourselves—quick, painless. Fail, and we keep you. You'll learn to sing with us. Forever."

Nara dove before she could think. The water took her like a lover's embrace, cold and absolute. As she swam toward the distant coast, she realized the deepkin hadn't bound her with rope or curse—only with choice. That, somehow, was crueler.

She would steal the charts. She had no choice but to try.

stories.title: The Starlight Cartographer's Reckoning
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer stands in his study as a new constellation blazes overhead—brighter, hotter, and utterly wrong. The maps he drew after the Celestial Ascension have begun to shift on their own, ink rearranging to show ruins he'd never recorded. His hands tremble as he realizes the pre-human structures aren't merely awakening to the new starlight—they're communicating through the stars themselves, projecting images of a catastrophe so ancient and vast it predates even the world's memory. In the Bone Wastes, his expedition team reports that the ruins now sing at dusk, their voices harmonizing with a distant humming from beneath the earth. Mardoc knows he's uncovered something that cannot be unmapped, cannot be forgotten. The Celestial Ascension was supposed to be an ending—a solution. Instead, it seems to have been an opening.

stories.title: Vex's Fragment Market in Collapse
stories.text: The shadow-markets of Anthrim are in chaos. Ever since the Celestial Ascension rewrote reality, the fragments Vex the Echo-Merchant trades in have become unstable—some carrying echoes of the cosmic working, others simply dissolving into smoke. Vex's cursed flesh aches with recognition she cannot articulate; her fragmented memory screams that she was *present* during the working, that she witnessed something her mind refuses to hold. When a desperate customer arrives seeking a fragment rumored to restore the memories stolen by the Ascension, Vex realizes she must venture into the Sunburned Reach—where merchants vanished, where the Ascension's consequences burned deepest. The journey will require her to confront not just the transformed landscape, but her own shattered past and the possibility that her curse is not punishment but *mercy*, keeping her from knowledge that would destroy her.

stories.title: The Deepwarden's Vigil
stories.text: Warden Sylas receives reports that defy understanding: scout-singers sent to investigate the destabilized Cerulean Trench are returning changed—their eyes reflecting light that does not exist, their speech fragmented into languages older than the deepkin kingdoms. The humming that echoes from the Trench has grown louder, and seismic tremors shake the cavern halls where the deepkin dwell. Sylas convenes the Council of Depths and learns a terrible truth: the Celestial Ascension's magic has fractured the barrier between the surface world and the Abyss Below. Something vast is stirring in the trenches, something the pre-human civilizations sealed away millennia ago. The deepkin must decide whether to seal themselves away completely, risk catastrophic war with the surface dwellers, or undertake an expedition to understand what awakens beneath. Sylas chooses the hardest path: descent into the Trench himself, seeking answers before war becomes inevitable.

stories.title: The Young Sorcerer's New Inheritance
stories.text: The apprentice sorcerer receives word from the Master Mage's scattered adherents: ancient texts hidden in the Tower are now readable under the new starlight, revealing knowledge the Master deliberately obscured. These texts describe the Celestial Ascension in exacting detail—and suggest it was not the first working of its kind. Previous civilizations performed similar rituals, and each one left behind a void in reality that eventually collapsed inward, consuming everything. The Master knew. The apprentice realizes their entire education was preparation not just to inherit power, but to *survive* the aftermath of Zareth's working. Now, as refugees flee the Sunburned Reach and omens darken the sky, the apprentice must decide: hide these dangerous texts or risk sharing forbidden knowledge that might unite the fractured kingdoms—or destroy them.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Dirge
stories.text: Kess of the Sunken Hollows has kept to her ruins since the plague took her village, singing to keep the dead at rest. But the new stars have changed the magic of bone and death itself. The corpses that she spent years lulling into stillness are stirring, their bones humming in resonance with the cosmic arrangement above. Refugees fleeing the Sunburned Reach arrive seeking her aid—they carry plague-marks, star-scars, and stories of merchants who transformed into living light before vanishing entirely. Kess realizes the Celestial Ascension didn't merely change the stars; it changed the fundamental agreement between the living and the dead. Her curse—the one that forces her to sing laments for every death she witnesses—now carries new weight: she must sing to keep not just the recently dead at peace, but the ancient dead whose sepulchres are resonating with starlight. If she fails, the bones will wake. And if they wake, the Sunken Hollows will become a necropolis of the Ascension's own making.

stories.title: The Refugee Caravan's Price
stories.text: Commodore Vesta of the Jade Coast receives refugees from the transformed Sunburned Reach—survivors of the Ascension's aftermath who describe cities unmade, trade routes swallowed by reality-shifts, and merchants who simply ceased to exist. Among them is a young archivist carrying a single ledger: records of the Sovereign's own dealings with Zareth before the Ascension. Vesta agrees to smuggle the archivist to safety, but discovers that the ledger itself is cursed—anyone who reads it gains fragments of Zareth's stolen memories, experiences that don't belong to them. As Vesta's crew begins experiencing visions of cosmic working, of sacrifice, of a moment when the stars *died* before rebirth, she must navigate not just the treacherous Jade Coast but the question of whether these memories are gift or poison. Some knowledge, she's learning, was meant to stay forgotten.

stories.title: The Tax Collector's Reckoning
stories.text: Levian the auditor arrives at provincial towns expecting order, but finds chaos: the Celestial Ascension has fractured the certainty upon which the Empire's fiscal system rests. Merchants in the Sunburned Reach who should have paid their levies have vanished. Trade caravans that crossed the region are reporting missing weeks—entire journeys erased or reordered in their memories. Levian meets with the Empire's High Steward and learns a secret: Zareth, before performing the Ascension, borrowed heavily against the Sovereign's treasury to fund his seven-year search. The Empire's wealth is partially *invested* in cosmic working now—and if the Ascension's consequences cascade into true catastrophe, the Empire's certainty of power and prosperity will collapse along with it. Levian accepts a new commission: travel the destabilized regions, catalog what was lost, and determine whether the Empire can salvage control or must adapt to a world where order is no longer guaranteed.

stories.title: The Hermit Woman's New Census
stories.text: The Hermit Woman of the Grey Wastes continues her endless count of the living and the dead, but her tally is corrupted. Some people she's counted are now *not*—erased by the Ascension. Others exist in places she's never traveled, occupying lives they shouldn't have. The hermit realizes the Celestial Ascension didn't merely change the stars; it changed the *census itself*—reality has been subtly rewritten, and her gift to remember every soul has become a curse of impossible contradiction. She walks the Grey Wastes with her ledgers, seeking the edges where the rewrite is most obvious, hoping to understand what layer of existence was replaced. What she discovers in the deep reaches of the wastes challenges everything she understands about the boundary between alive and dead, counted and uncounted. She begins to understand that Zareth's sacrifice wasn't payment—it was a key, and something far older is waking to use what he unlocked.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Starborn Debt
stories.text: Skarath the wyvern, ancient collector of debts and promises, circles the new constellation and feels something fundamentally altered in the cosmic order—a debt that has been *paid*, but not by any creature he knows. The Ascension has created a rupture in the agreement between the sky and the earth, and Skarath's ability to track and enforce ancient oaths is fracturing. He descends to the cavern halls to consult with Skyrakis the dragon, seeking understanding of what new powers now move in the heavens. Together, they discover that the new stars are *alive*—conscious, aware, and operating under obligations none of the older powers negotiated. If the dragons and wyverns are to maintain their place in the world's hierarchy, they must make new pacts with these starborn entities. Skarath undertakes a negotiation unlike any in recorded history: bargaining not with kings or sorcerers, but with the light itself—trying to ensure the debts of the old world still matter in whatever comes next.
